---


---

<h1 id="margin-trading">Margin Trading</h1>
<p>Margin trading gives you the ability to enter into positions larger than your account balance.</p>
<h2 id="what-is-account-balance">What is account balance?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Risk Capital</strong></mark> =&gt; cash you can afford to lose</li>
<li><mark><strong>Account Balance/Balance</strong></mark> =&gt; is the starting balance of your account
<ul>
<li>it’s the amount of cash in your account</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<p><strong><code>Your balance will only change in one of three ways:</code></strong></p>
<ol>
<li>When you  add more funds  to your account.</li>
<li>When you  close a position.</li>
<li>When you keep a position open overnight and either receive or pay  swap/rollover fee.</li>
</ol>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Rollover</strong></mark> =&gt; the procedure of moving open positions from one trading day to another
<ul>
<li>most brokers perform the rollover  automatically  by closing any open positions at the end of the day, while simultaneously opening an identical position for the following business day</li>
<li>during this rollover, a  swap  is calculated</li>
<li>a  swap is a fee that is either  paid or charged  to you at the end of each trading day if you keep your trade open overnight</li>
<li>if you are paid swap, cash will be  added  to your balance</li>
<li>if you are charged swap, cash will be  deducted  from your balance</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="what-is-unrealized-pl-and-floating-pl">What is unrealized P/L and floating P/L?</h2>
<p>When trading, there are actually two different types of “profit or loss”, also known as “P/L”.</p>
<h3 id="unrealized-pl">Unrealized P/L</h3>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Unrealized P/L</strong></mark> =&gt;  refers to the profit or loss held in your current open positions….your currently active trades</li>
<li>this is equal to the profit or loss that would be “realized” if all your open positions were closed  immediately</li>
<li>unrealized P/L is also known as “Floating P/L” because the value is constantly changing since your positions are still open</li>
</ul>
<p><strong><code>Example: Floating Loss</code></strong></p>
<p>Let’s say your account is in USD and you are currently  long  10,000 units  EUR/USD, which was bought at  1.15000.</p>
<p>The current exchange rate for EUR/USD is  1.13000.</p>
<p>Let’s calculate the position’s Floating P/L:</p>
<p><code>Floating P/L = Position Size x (Current Price - Entry Price)</code><br>
<code>Floating P/L = 10,000 x (1.13000 - 1.15000)</code><br>
<code>-200 = 10,000 x (- 0.0200)</code></p>
<p>The position is  down  200 pips.</p>
<p>Since you’re trading a mini lot, each pip is worth $1.</p>
<p>So you currently have a  Floating Loss of $200 (200 pips x $1).</p>
<h3 id="realized-pl">Realized P/L</h3>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Realized P/L</strong></mark> =&gt; profit or loss that comes from a completed trade</li>
<li>your profits or losses only become realized when the positions are closed</li>
<li>this is the only time when your account balance will change to reflect any gains or losses</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="what-is-margin">What is margin?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Margin</strong></mark> =&gt; a small amount of capital to open and maintain a new position</li>
<li>it is simply a portion of your funds that your forex broker sets aside from your account balance to keep your trade open and to ensure that you can cover the potential loss of the trade</li>
<li>once the trade is closed, the margin is “released” back into your account and can now be “usable” again to open new trades</li>
</ul>
<h3 id="what-is-margin-requirement">What is margin requirement?</h3>
<ul>
<li>margin is expressed as a percentage (%) (this percentage is known as the margin requirement) of the “full position size”, also known as the “Notional Value” of the position you wish to open</li>
<li><mark><strong>Notional Value</strong></mark> =&gt; total value of the position you want to open</li>
</ul>
<h3 id="what-is-required-margin">What is required margin?</h3>
<ul>
<li>when margin is expressed as a specific amount of your account’s currency, this amount is known as the required margin</li>
<li><strong>Required Margin</strong> is also known as <strong>Deposit Margin</strong>, <strong>Entry Margin</strong>, or <strong>Initial Margin</strong></li>
</ul>
<h3 id="example">Example:</h3>
<ul>
<li>let’s say you’ve deposited $1,000 in your account and want to go long USD/JPYand want to open 1 mini lot (10,000 units) position</li>
<li>since USD is the base currency. this mini lot is 10,000 dollars, which means the position’s  Notional Value is $10,000</li>
<li>assuming your trading account is denominated in USD, since the  Margin Requirement is 4%, the  Required Margin will be $400</li>
</ul>
<p><img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Alt text"></p>
<h3 id="how-to-calculate-required-margin">How to calculate required margin?</h3>
<ul>
<li>when trading with margin, the  amount of margin  (“Required Margin”) needed to hold open a position is calculated as a  percentage (“Margin Requirement”) of the  position size  (“Notional Value”)</li>
<li>the specific amount of Required Margin is calculated according to the base currency of the currency pair traded.</li>
<li>if the  base currency is DIFFERENT from your trading account’s currency, the Required Margin is then converted to your  account denomination</li>
</ul>
<p><code>If the base currency is the SAME as your account’s currency:</code></p>
<p>Required Margin = Notional Value x Margin Requirement</p>
<p><code>If the base currency is DIFFERENT from your account’s currency:</code></p>
<p>Required Margin = Notional Value x Margin Requirement x Exchange Rate Between Base Currency and Account Currency</p>
<h2 id="what-is-used-margin">What is used margin?</h2>
<ul>
<li>if you open more than one position at a time, each specific position will have its own Required Margin</li>
<li><mark><strong>Used Margin</strong></mark> =&gt; is all the margin that’s “locked up” and can’t be used to open new positions (the sum of all required margins being used)</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="what-is-equity">What is equity?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Equity</strong></mark> =&gt; is the current value of the account and  fluctuates  with every  tick  when looking at your trading platform on your screen</li>
<li>it is the sum of your account balance and all floating (unrealized) profits or losses associated with your open positions</li>
<li>as your current trades rise or fall in value, so does your Equity</li>
<li>shows the “TEMPORARY” value of your account at the current time (floating account balance)</li>
<li>reflects the real-time calculation of your profit/loss</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="what-is-free-margin">What is free margin?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Free Margin</strong></mark> =&gt; is the difference between Equity and Used Margin</li>
<li><strong>Free Margin</strong> is also known as <strong>Usable Margin</strong>, <strong>Usable Maintenance Margin</strong>, <strong>Available Margin</strong>, and “<strong>Available to Trade</strong>“</li>
<li><code>Free Margin = Equity - Used Margin</code></li>
</ul>
<h2 id="what-is-margin-level">What is margin level?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Margin Level</strong></mark> =&gt; is the percentage (%) value based on the amount of Equity versus Used Margin</li>
<li>Margin Level allows you to know how much of your funds are available for new trades</li>
<li>the higher the Margin Level, the more Free Margin you have available to trade</li>
<li>the lower the Margin Level, the less Free Margin available to trade</li>
<li><code>Margin Level = (Equity / Used Margin) x 100%</code></li>
<li>forex brokers use margin levels to determine whether you can open additional positions</li>
<li>different brokers set different Margin Level limits, but most brokers set this limit at 100%</li>
<li>this means that when your Equity is equal to or less than your Used Margin, you will NOT be able to open any new positions</li>
<li>if the Margin Level is 100% or less, <em>most</em> trading platforms will not allow you to open new trades</li>
</ul>
<p><img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Alt text"></p>
<h2 id="what-is-a-margin-call">What is a margin call?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Margin Call</strong></mark> =&gt; when your broker notifies you that your Margin Level has fallen below the required minimum level (the “Margin Call Level”)</li>
<li>in forex trading, the  Margin Call Level  is when the  Margin Level  has reached a  specific  level or threshold</li>
<li>when this threshold is reached, you are in danger of the POSSIBILITY of having some or all of your positions forcibly closed (or “liquidated“)</li>
<li>the Margin Level is the “metric” and the “Margin Call  <em>Level</em>” is a specific “value” of the metric (which is the Margin Level)</li>
<li>a Margin Call occurs when your  floating losses are greater than your Used Margin</li>
<li>this means that your Equity is less than your Used Margin (since  floating losses  reduce your Equity)</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="what-is-a-stop-out-level">What is a stop out level?</h2>
<ul>
<li><mark><strong>Stop Out Level</strong></mark> =&gt; when your  Margin Level falls to a specific percentage (%) level in which one or all of your open positions are closed automatically (“liquidated”) by your broker</li>
<li>this liquidation happens because the trading account can no longer support the open positions due to a lack of margin</li>
<li>the Stop Out Level is when the  Equity is lower than a specific percentage of your Used Margin</li>
<li>if this level is reached, your broker will automatically start closing out your trades starting with the most unprofitable one until your Margin Level is back above the Stop Out Level</li>
<li>this act of closing your positions is called a <strong>Stop Out</strong></li>
<li>the <strong>Stop Out Level</strong> is also known as the <strong>Margin Closeout Value, Liquidation Margin, or Minimum Required Margin</strong></li>
<li>each position that is closed “releases” Used Margin, which increases your Margin Level</li>
<li>but if closing this position is still not enough to get back the Margin Level above 20%, your broker will continue to close positions until it does</li>
<li>the Stop Out Level is meant to prevent you from losing more money than you have deposited</li>
<li>if your trade continued to keep losing, eventually, you’d have no more money in your account and you’d end up with a  negative account balance =&gt; this prevents negative account balance</li>
</ul>
<h2 id="the-relationship-between-margin-and-leverage">The relationship between margin and leverage</h2>
<ul>
<li>you use margin to create  leverage</li>
<li>leverage is the increased “trading power” that is available when using a margin account</li>
<li>leverage allows you to trade positions LARGER than the amount of money in your trading account</li>
<li>leverage is expressed as a ratio</li>
<li>leverage is the ratio between the amount of money you really have and the amount of money you can trade</li>
<li><code>Leverage = 1 / Margin Requirement</code></li>
</ul>
<p><code>Here are examples of Leverage Ratios depending on the Margin Requirement:</code></p>
<p><img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Alt text"></p>
<blockquote>
<p><strong>Forex Margin vs. Securities Margin</strong></p>
<p>Forex margin and securities margin are two very different things.<br>
Understanding the difference is important.</p>
<p>In the securities world, margin is the money you  <strong>borrow</strong>  as a<br>
partial down payment, usually up to 50% of the purchase price, to buy<br>
and own a stock, bond, or ETF.</p>
<p>This practice is often referred to as “<strong>buying on margin”</strong>.</p>
<p>So if you’re trading stocks on margin, you’re borrowing money from<br>
your stockbroker to purchase stock. Basically, a  <strong>loan</strong>  from the<br>
brokerage firm.</p>
<p>In the forex market, margin is the amount of money that you must<br>
deposit and keep on hand with your trading platform when you open a<br>
position.</p>
<p>It is NOT a down payment and you do NOT own the underlying currency<br>
pair.</p>
<p>Margin can be looked at as a  <strong>good faith deposit</strong>  or<br>
<strong>collateral</strong>  that’s used to ensure each party (buyer and seller) can meet their obligations of the agreement.</p>
<p>Unlike margin in stock trading, margin <strong>in forex trading is not<br>
borrowed money</strong>.</p>
<p>When trading forex,<br>
nothing is actually being bought or sold, only the  <strong>agreement</strong>  (or<br>
contract) to buy or sell is exchanged, so borrowing is not needed.</p>
<p>The term “margin” is used across multiple financial markets. However,<br>
there is a difference between how margin is used when trading<br>
securities versus when trading forex.<br>
Understanding this difference is essential prior to trading forex.`</p>
</blockquote>
